input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tooling and enablers include Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for system modeling, architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component-connector-based system descriptions, MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines for behavioral modeling, MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools for simulation, general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java, and methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to manage complexity through layered abstraction levels.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The computational models include structural models using Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs to define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition; behavioral models via MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines; and analytical models utilizing MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ or Java. Data sources originate from contextual data traces of physical objects, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes. Data management involves integrating heterogeneous modeling notations and tools across stakeholder perspectives. Model relationships are defined through component connectors and interfaces in ADLs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis with current (IM) as input, utilizing a gear transmission system comprising a standard gear stage followed by a cycloidal gear to achieve the required low gear ratio.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft and a secondary encoder located behind the gearbox, providing precise position feedback with high resolution and reliability.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated by comparing simulated data against measured data to analyze friction behavior influenced by load and temperature variations. Uncertainty sources include environmental factors affecting friction coefficients, with error detection based on deviations between simulation and physical measurements.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors to the digital system via MQTT protocol, transmitting time-series data including target and actual position values, their derivatives, and motor current measurements. No details on transmission frequencies, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication via MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming, with Asset Administration Shells (AAS) utilized for asset management and interoperability. Network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability mechanisms, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle stages are structured through Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with distinct abstraction levels including requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer. Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT manage complexity transitions. Governance integrates heterogeneous stakeholders across modeling notations (SysML, ADLs) and tools (Simulink, C++, Java), with sequential steps: problem identification, asset and model analysis, use or creation of calculations/models, and data identification.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for mechanical structure modeling, implementation of Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for asset metadata, and live-data linking for real-time synchronization. Evolution is managed through a dynamic pipeline for friction model exchange (static, kinetic, viscous parameters), following sequential steps of problem identification, asset analysis, model creation, and data identification. Validation incorporates simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose code (C++, Java) for continuous refinement.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support capabilities include deriving friction parameters (static, kinetic, and viscous) within gearboxes to enhance robot control accuracy. Analytics process sensor data to generate actionable insights, with user interaction facilitated through integrated visualization within a unified dashboard for monitoring and optimizing physical system performance.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance includes SysML for Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), Unified Modeling Language (UML) for reference model diagrams (Fig. 1), and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for digital asset representation. Interoperability is achieved via Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as AADL and MontiArc, adhering to manufacturing and industrial automation standards.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical system. Section III identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and details its components including a 'permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', and the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the text describes specific capabilities of the digital shadow. The Abstract mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' and enabling the 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' to enhance robot motion accuracy. These correspond to visualization and monitoring services.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed list of tools and methodologies that is strongly supported by Section II.A of the source text. It accurately captures the MBSE languages, ADLs, and simulation tools mentioned. Quotes: 'architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs' and 'executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions 'real-time' data and 'live measurements' but does not provide specific details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity. Quote: 'Our approach leverages digital shadows — real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior'.

The document describes the creation of a single digital shadow instance for the first axis of a robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. Quote: 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow.'

The text focuses on the internal structure and modeling of a specific digital shadow instance using a reference model. It does not describe the overall orchestration or coordination of a larger Digital Twin system constellation. Quote: 'In the following section, we build an executable digital shadow whose data is displayed in a dashboard.'

The paper mentions MQTT and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) but only in the context of streaming data from the specific robot to its shadow/dashboard. It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards with other systems, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins. Quote: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.'

Therefore, the claim that it is not in the document is accurate.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions a 'safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range', this is a description of a physical mechanical constraint (a hardware limit switch or cage) rather than a cybersecurity protocol, access control mechanism, or safety-critical system consideration in the context of digital twin security. There is no discussion of cybersecurity, authentication, threat mitigation, or risk assessment strategies relevant to the digital shadow's implementation.
